        private readonly AppIdToProductResolver inner;
        private readonly ILogger<FallbackAppIdToProductResolver> logger;

        /// <summary>
        /// Initializes a new instance of the <see cref="FallbackAppIdToProductResolver"/> class.
        /// </summary>
        /// <param name="inner">The real resolver to delegate to first.</param>
        /// <param name="logger">Logger.</param>
        public FallbackAppIdToProductResolver(
            AppIdToProductResolver inner,
            ILogger<FallbackAppIdToProductResolver> logger)
        {
            this.inner = inner ?? throw new ArgumentNullException(nameof(inner));
            this.logger = logger ?? throw new ArgumentNullException(nameof(logger));
        }

        /// <inheritdoc/>
        public async Task<IReadOnlyDictionary<string, string>> ResolveAsync(CancellationToken cancellationToken)
        {
            // Delegate to the real resolver first.
            var liveResult = await this.inner.ResolveAsync(cancellationToken);

            // Copy into a mutable dictionary so we can backfill.
            var result = new Dictionary<string, string>(liveResult, StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase);

            // Backfill any missing entries from the hardcoded fallback snapshot.
            var backfillCount = 0;
            foreach (var kvp in FallbackMappings)
            {
                if (result.TryAdd(kvp.Key, kvp.Value))
                {
                    backfillCount++;
                }
            }

            if (backfillCount > 0)
            {
                this.logger.LogInformation(
                    "FallbackAppIdToProductResolver backfilled {BackfillCount} AppId→Product mappings from fallback snapshot. Total: {TotalCount}.",
                    backfillCount,
                    result.Count);
            }

            return result;
        }
    }

content.text:
Here is the complete code review based on the git history analysis:

---

## Code Review: PR #6501814

**Summary:** This PR removes `FallbackAppIdToProductResolver` and its supporting infrastructure (old `ServiceTreeClient`, authentication modes, etc.), simplifies `ServiceTreeClientOptions`, and directly registers `AppIdToProductResolver` as the live implementation. The TODO comment in the code explicitly gated this on Kusto reader permissions being confirmed.

---

### Issue 1 — Double-logging on Kusto query failure

**Files:** `ServiceTreeKustoClient.cs` (lines ~130–140 and ~283–295)

**Description:** When `QueryHierarchyBatchAsync` throws a non-cancellation exception, it now logs at `Error` level *and* rethrows. The caller (`GetHierarchyNodesByServiceIdsAsync`) then catches that same exception and logs it again at `Warning` level. Every Kusto batch failure will produce two log entries — one `Error` and one `Warning` — for the same underlying failure.

**Historical context:** In the base commit (`1e13720b`), `QueryHierarchyBatchAsync` caught exceptions, logged at `Warning`, and returned an empty result *without* rethrowing. The outer batch task had *no* catch block at all (just a `finally`). The new PR added a proper outer catch, which is a genuine improvement — but it didn't remove the rethrow inside `QueryHierarchyBatchAsync`, leaving both handlers active.

**Why flagged:** This is a real behavioral regression in observability. A single Kusto connection failure will fire an `Error`-level alert (which may page on-call engineers) in addition to the expected `Warning`. The outer `LogWarning` message (`"Batch of {BatchCount} IDs failed; continuing with remaining batches"`) is the appropriate signal here; the `LogError` + rethrow inside the inner method is redundant and misleading since the exception is subsequently swallowed.

---

### Issue 2 — Permanent negative caching eliminates any recovery path if Kusto is down at startup

**Files:** `ServiceTreeKustoClient.cs` / `ServiceTreeParentResolver.cs` (line 118), `CodexSite.cs` (line 112), `Startup.cs` (line 130)

**Description:** `ServiceTreeParentResolver` is a **Singleton** that permanently caches `UnknownParent` for any Service Tree ID that can't be resolved (line 118: `this.cache[id] = UnknownParent`). If Kusto is unavailable when the service starts (or when `AppIdToProductResolver` is first called), every ID will be cached as unresolvable for the lifetime of the process. A process restart is the only recovery path.

**Historical context:** The `FallbackAppIdToProductResolver` was introduced in commit `06240c81` (Mar 11, 2026) specifically because Kusto reader permissions were *not yet confirmed working* for the multi-tenant app. Its inline TODO comment read:

> *"TODO: Remove this entire class once Kusto Reader permissions are confirmed working for the multi-tenant app (3696e88e-d200-4d2c-9503-c503b9c4547f) on the ServiceTree Kusto cluster."*

The fallback contained a hardcoded snapshot (snapshotted on 2026-03-11) of ~195 `QuotaAppId → Product` mappings. Those mappings ensured the GPU utilization metrics pipeline produced correct `ProductOrg` values even if Kusto was down. Removing the fallback leaves zero resilience against a Kusto outage.

**Why flagged:** The commit message for the fallback was *"hardcoded service tree to unblock if permission doesn't work"* — it was a deliberate safety net. The PR removes it without any evidence in the commit history (the surrounding commits are titled "error handling", "logs and throwing", "dead code clean up") that the permissions were actually verified in production. If Kusto reader permissions fail silently at startup, all `AppIdToProduct` mappings will resolve to empty and `UtilizationMetricBackgroundService` will report blank `ProductOrg` for all GPU utilization data — a silent data quality regression with no alerting.

---

### Issue 3 — 12 AppIds mapped to non-catalog product names in the fallback have no replacement path

**Files:** `FallbackAppIdToProductResolver.cs` (deleted), `ServiceTreeParentGroupCatalog.cs`

**Description:** The deleted `FallbackAppIdToProductResolver` contained 12 entries under the comment `// Non-catalog ServiceGroupName fallbacks` that mapped AppIds to product names that **do not exist** in `ServiceTreeParentGroupCatalog`: `"Relevance & Sciences"`, `"CoreAI - Responsible AI - RAI (SLBird)"`, `"Phi Service Group"`, `"Xandr"`, and `"Advertising Supply Platform"` (the last one does appear in the catalog, but as `"Ads"`, not the exact string). The affected AppIds include `Sydney`, `turing`, `turing-cdex`, `TuringBot`, `RAI`, `PhiDev`, and `MonetizeImaqSupplyPolicyEnforcement`.

**Historical context:** These entries were deliberately hardcoded with non-standard product names (e.g., `"Relevance & Sciences"` instead of `"Bing"`). The catalog (`ServiceTreeParentGroupCatalog`) normalizes everything into ~5 products (`Ads`, `Bing`, `MSN`, `Copilot`). If the live Kusto hierarchy for these AppIds resolves to a group not in the catalog, `ServiceTreeParentResolver.MatchFromHierarchyNode` returns a `ParentServiceGroupInfo` with an empty `Product`, and `AppIdToProductResolver` then *excludes that AppId entirely* from the output (it checks `!string.IsNullOrEmpty(parent.Product)`). The `FallbackAppIdToProductResolver` was the safety net ensuring these would still appear in GPU utilization dashboards with their intended grouping labels.

**Why flagged:** The fallback's non-catalog entries were added intentionally for services whose Service Tree hierarchy doesn't match the catalog IDs/names. Removing the fallback without verifying that Kusto returns catalog-matching hierarchy data for these specific IDs risks silently dropping `Sydney`, `turing`, `RAI`, `PhiDev`, and related services from `GpuUtilizationByModelOrgRow` tables.

---

### Issue 4 — Inconsistent exception-handling contract between inner and outer methods creates confusing error semantics

**Files:** `ServiceTreeKustoClient.cs` (lines ~280–295 vs. lines ~130–140)

**Description:** `QueryHierarchyBatchAsync` now rethrows all non-cancellation exceptions after logging. However, its caller immediately catches and swallows those same exceptions (logging `Warning`). This creates a contract where `QueryHierarchyBatchAsync` *claims* to be a throwing method (by rethrowing) but is *effectively* a swallowing method as observed from outside. Future callers who add a new use site of `QueryHierarchyBatchAsync` and don't wrap it in a `try/catch` will be surprised by exceptions surfacing that the existing callers never see.

**Historical context:** The pre-PR `QueryHierarchyBatchAsync` (already present in the base commit) was consistent: it swallowed all exceptions internally and always returned a (possibly empty) dictionary. The change to rethrow was introduced in commit `bfe33cd5` ("error handling") and `bb8ff72f` ("logs and throwing") earlier in this PR branch, but then the outer catch was added in commit `b6260047`, leaving both behaviors active simultaneously.

**Why flagged:** The pattern of log-error-then-rethrow where the exception is guaranteed to be caught and swallowed one level up is an anti-pattern that degrades log signal-to-noise and creates misleading method contracts.
